bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Early English Poems, Transactions of the Philological Society 1858, Part II (Berlin: A. Asher & Co., 1862), pp. 1–180.
bibliographic.publisher: A. Asher & Co. for the Philological Society
bibliographic.title: Early English Poems

entities.description: The immediate source-edition state represented by the XML. Its encoded preliminaries read 'The Early South-English Legendary or Lives of Saints' and 'Transactions of the Philological Society 1858'; DIMEV identifies Furnivall's part as Berlin 1862. The text comprises thirty-six selected items on pp. 1–161. The precise physical issue exemplar used for encoding remains unidentified.
entities.label: Transactions of the Philological Society 1858, Part II: Early English Poems
notes: The XML selection ends at p. 161; Furnivall's appended comparative text and apparatus continue through p. 180.
The issue's volume year and the part's publication year are recorded separately rather than collapsed.

rights.basis: The selected textual works are directly attested in thirteenth- through fifteenth-century manuscripts, centuries before the modern copyright term. This work-level conclusion is independent of U-M's more conservative No Copyright–United States provider statement and does not govern modern photographs, catalog text, editions, markup, or provider interfaces.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English and Latin textual content represented in the edition

rights.basis: This issue-state edition and its apparatus were published in Berlin in 1862, despite the containing Transactions volume year 1858. The publication date independently places the nineteenth-century edition material in the United States public domain; this claim does not govern later encodings or reproductions.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1862 Transactions issue-state edition text and editorial apparatus

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Early English Poems and Lives of Saints (with Those of the Wicked Birds Pilate and Judas) (Berlin: A. Asher & Co. for the Philological Society, 1862).
bibliographic.publisher: A. Asher & Co. for the Philological Society
bibliographic.title: Early English Poems and Lives of Saints (with Those of the Wicked Birds Pilate and Judas)

entities.description: The complete standalone presentation preserved by the Internet Archive exemplar: title page, preface signed 17 December 1861, contents, corrigenda, selected texts on pp. 1–161, notes on pp. 162–165, index on pp. 166–171, and list of words on pp. 172–180. Its main text corresponds to the issue-state selection, but its preliminary leaves differ from the XML.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, Early English Poems and Lives of Saints (1862 standalone title-page state)

rights.basis: The standalone title-page state and its editorial apparatus were published in Berlin in 1862, independently placing the nineteenth-century edition material in the United States public domain. This claim does not govern later encodings or scan derivatives.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1862 edition text and editorial apparatus

access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 913

access.accessMethod: Request Harley MS 913 through the British Library collection item request route and consult in London with a current Reader Pass.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 913

entities.description: The Kildare Lyrics codex and Furnivall's direct source for items 1–7 and 32–36. The selected pieces occupy discontiguous loci across ff. 3–63; Harley 913 is one physical witness, not evidence that the selected works share one author.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 913

physicalDescription.format: Codex in Gothic cursive; Middle English, Old French, and Latin
notes: Probably produced in south-east Ireland, perhaps Waterford, in a Franciscan context.

access.entityLabel: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Harley MS 913
notes: No local_copies entry: no bulk copy was created because the exact manifest points to a rights URL that returned HTTP 404 and no reusable bulk-download license or permission was established.
The first and last Image API URLs are working unauthenticated boundary images for the selected Harley 913 range, f. 3r and f. 63v. The manifest canvas IDs themselves returned HTTP 400.
The complete object remains publicly available online despite the no-cache decision.

access.accessMethod: Request Egerton MS 613 through the British Library collection item request route and consult in London with a current Reader Pass.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Egerton MS 613

dateStatements.label: Relevant manuscript sections
dateStatements.value: Thirteenth century; later copy in the first half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: A composite thirteenth-century miscellany containing two copies of the Conduct of Life. Furnivall printed the earlier copy at ff. 7r–12v and recorded readings from the later incomplete copy at ff. 64r–70v; both belong to one codex but have distinct scribal and chronological contexts.
entities.label: British Library, Egerton MS 613

physicalDescription.format: Composite codex copied by eleven scribes
notes: Probable south-west Midlands origin; the principal copy is ff. 7r–12v and the later incomplete copy is ff. 64r–70v.

access.entityLabel: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Egerton MS 613
notes: No local_copies entry: no bulk copy was created because the manifest says only 'Public Domain in most countries, other than the UK' and supplies neither a reusable license nor clear bulk-download permission.
The four working Image API URLs bound the principal copy at ff. 7r–12v and later collation copy at ff. 64r–70v. The corresponding manifest canvas IDs returned HTTP 400.
The complete object remains publicly available online despite the no-cache decision.

rights.basis: The exact manifest says 'Public Domain in most countries, other than the UK' but supplies no license. The territorial statement is preserved without converting it into unrestricted reuse permission.

access.accessMethod: Order digital reference or reproduction images through British Library Digitisation Services, supplying the exact shelfmark and pencil-foliation target loci; manuscript originals may require expert photography and every order is subject to condition, feasibility, quote, payment, and use terms.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 2277
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction images, or expert manuscript photography, as approved
notes: For the CME00116 source run, request both sides and adjacent leaves for all Furnivall-selected legends and miracles between ff. 51 and 232v, and ask the Library to confirm the current pencil-foliation crosswalk before photography.
DIMEV's Pilate locus through f. 234 conflicts with the current cataloged 232-folio extent; preserve that conflict in the order rather than silently normalizing it.
Supply of images is not permission to reproduce them; obtain written, order-specific reuse permission where required.

entities.description: The c. 1300 South English Legendary witness from which Furnivall selected items 9–24. The manuscript is imperfect at the beginning; current catalog evidence places production in south-west England, and DIMEV/LALME associate its language with Somerset.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2277
notes: DIMEV reports Pilate through f. 234, which conflicts with the current cataloged physical extent through f. 232v.

physicalDescription.format: Codex in Gothic cursive; South English Legendary on ff. 1r–232v, imperfect at beginning
notes: Current catalog origin: south-west England.

access.entityLabel: British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)

access.accessMethod: Order digital reference or reproduction images through British Library Digitisation Services, supplying the exact shelfmark and pencil-foliation target loci; manuscript originals may require expert photography and every order is subject to condition, feasibility, quote, payment, and use terms.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction images, or expert manuscript photography, as approved
notes: For the CME00116 source run, request both sides and adjacent leaves across ff. 128v–130r, including complete columns and current pencil foliation so that Furnivall's six-song sequence can be checked without cropping away context.
Supply of images is not permission to reproduce them; obtain written, order-specific reuse permission where required.

entities.description: The late-fourteenth-century Simeon Manuscript and Furnivall's base for items 25–30 at ff. 128–130. It is closely related to the Vernon Manuscript and shares its principal scribe, but only item 25 was collated against Vernon.
entities.label: British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.format: Large religious and literary codex in Middle English, French, and Latin
notes: Probably produced in or associated with north Worcestershire; Scribe 2 also copied most of the Vernon Manuscript.

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)
access.format: Current MARCO and Medieval Manuscripts catalog records

entities.description: The c. 1390–1400 Vernon Manuscript. Its copy of the Song of Mercy at f. 407ra–c supplied Furnivall with four lines and variant readings for item 25 only. It is a collateral comparison witness, not the base of items 25–30.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: 8 + 342 surviving medieval folios plus modern leaves; original extent 8 + 413 folios
physicalDescription.format: Very large illustrated codex with Middle English, Anglo-Norman, and Latin texts
notes: Current catalog date c. 1390–1400 and probable West Midlands origin.
The Song of Mercy is f. 407ra–c.

access.entityLabel: ORA complete electronic facsimile edition of the Vernon Manuscript
access.format: Complete 4.3 GB ZIP electronic facsimile and transcription

localCopies.label: Complete ORA Vernon Manuscript electronic facsimile package
notes: The downloaded ZIP passed a complete archive integrity test with no compressed-data errors.
This ignored cache is the one local copy permitted for private study or research and noncommercial use; it must not be committed, redistributed, or treated as openly licensed.

workPortion.label: Vernon Song of Mercy comparison used for CME00116 item 25
locators: archive member: The Vernon MS - uuid 797a4c33-cb7b-4fbc-b939-2412769ead1f/resources/vernon/images/facsimile/200/vernon_407r.jpg (highest packaged image, 3,627,536 bytes)
archive member: The Vernon MS - uuid 797a4c33-cb7b-4fbc-b939-2412769ead1f/engine/volume/vernon/transcript/transcript_f_407r.html
notes: The complete facsimile package is cached even though the source-critical work portion is only the Song of Mercy comparison passage.
Equivalent f. 407v image and transcript members also exist in the package, but Furnivall's stated comparison locus is confined to f. 407ra–c.
The ORA package has no tested human-facing page-deep-link equivalent for the target locus.
Public online access and one complete local research copy are recorded; ORA's private-study/research, noncommercial-use, and no-redistribution limits remain controlling.
The work portion is the Song of Mercy at f. 407ra–c; the ORA package has no tested human-facing page-deep-link equivalent.

rights.basis: The ORA item names Bodleian Library Publishing as copyright holder, dated 2019. ORA's default terms allow one copy for private study or research for noncommercial purposes, do not authorize redistribution, and prohibit profit-making use absent another license.
rights.component: 2019 electronic facsimile, transcription, interface, and essays
notes: This statement is separate from the public-domain medieval text depicted.

entities.description: The mid-fifteenth-century witness of Why I Can't Be a Nun. The poem is imperfect at beginning and end across ff. 177–182, with a damaged continuation misbound as f. 190; the two catalog components belong to the same medieval manuscript text.

physicalDescription.format: Composite codex; the target occupies ff. 177–182 and misbound f. 190

next_steps: Ask U-M for source-copy catalog and imaging provenance.
Locate a complete Transactions 1858 Part II issue and compare preliminaries, signatures, pagination, and appended matter against the XML and cached scan.
Keep the volume year 1858 separate from the Berlin publication year 1862 until the issue history is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Transactions issue exemplar supplied the XML's half-title and 1858 title leaf, and what is its exact relationship to the standalone 1862 volume?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether legacy digitization survives and request working manifests or complete target runs.
If commissioned, include adjacent leaves, blanks, dislocated f. 190, bindings, and foliation evidence; obtain order-specific reuse terms.
Do not infer nondigitization from currently unavailable legacy viewers.
openQuestions.question: Can complete stable page-image runs with explicit research and reuse terms be obtained for Harley MS 2277, Add MS 22283, and Cotton MS Vespasian D IX?

next_steps: Inspect complete final-leaf images or request a current-versus-legacy foliation concordance from the British Library.
Compare Furnivall's printed text and any old foliation against DIMEV's source record before changing the locus.
openQuestions.question: How should DIMEV's Harley MS 2277 locus through f. 234 be reconciled with the current BL codex extent through f. 232v for Pilate?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to repair or replace Harley 913's manifest rights URL and clarify bulk-download permission.
Obtain a jurisdiction-specific reuse statement or license for Egerton 613 beyond the territorial public-domain note.
Continue using public viewer and tested Image API boundaries without bulk-caching until terms are clear.
openQuestions.question: What exact reusable license or permission applies to bulk local research copies of the complete Harley MS 913 and Egerton MS 613 IIIF objects?

reviewNotes: The lineage primary_subject is the repository artifact entity artifact:general-corpus:CME00116, which carries repository_file.path; no abstract work or source edition is used as the primary subject.

The five principal physical witnesses and the Vernon comparison witness remain separate. Item 8's two Egerton copies are separately scoped within one codex; item 31's dislocated f. 190 remains part of the Cotton witness.

Complete public access and local caching are separate assertions: Harley 913 and Egerton 613 are online and complete but remain uncached because reusable bulk-download permission was not established; Vernon is cached as the one private-study/research copy allowed by ORA terms and remains non-redistributable.
Every cached complete source object records the corpus work portion and tested boundaries where a human-facing deep link exists.

lineage.summary: CME00116 is a repository copy of U-M's electronic encoding of Furnivall's thirty-six-item edited selection. The XML represents a Transactions 1858 Part II issue state published in Berlin in 1862; a complete cached Internet Archive scan preserves the related standalone 1862 title-page state and full apparatus. Furnivall transcribed items 1–7 and 32–36 from Harley MS 913, item 8 from one Egerton MS 613 copy with the later copy collated, items 9–24 from Harley MS 2277, items 25–30 from Add MS 22283 with Vernon used only for item 25, and item 31 from Cotton MS Vespasian D IX. A complete ORA Vernon facsimile is cached under ORA's one-copy private-study/research terms; complete public BL IIIF objects for Harley 913 and Egerton 613 remain uncached because reusable bulk-download permission was not established.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English poets and compilers
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts attested from the thirteenth to mid-fifteenth century; editorial compilation published 1862
catalogSummary.region: England and south-east Ireland; witness dialect regions include the south-west Midlands, Somerset, north Worcestershire, and Waterford
catalogSummary.title: Early English Poems and Lives of Saints

catalogingSubject.label: Early English Poems and Lives of Saints
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Frederick J. Furnivall's editorially selected and ordered anthology of thirty-six numbered Middle English verse items, represented by the repository XML as the medieval-text portion of Transactions of the Philological Society 1858, Part II. The selection draws directly on five principal physical witnesses and uses a sixth witness only to collate item 25. It is not one medieval authored work, one saint's life, one manuscript, the whole South English Legendary, or the complete 1862 printed volume. Medieval components, editorial compilation, issue and standalone title-page states, digital encoding, repository artifact, witnesses, and facsimiles remain distinct layers.

contentParts.label: 1. A Sarmun
notes: Sixty four-line groups; the edition splits number 2 into 2a and 2b.
contentParts.summary: A moral sermon against pride and covetousness, on death, hell, and heaven.

contentParts.label: 2. XV Signa ante Iudicium (fragment)
notes: The XML uses one LG for 180 lines; printed pp. 162–164 add a comparison text that is outside the encoded work boundary.
contentParts.summary: A fragmentary Middle English account of the Fifteen Signs before Judgment, ending during the twelfth day.

contentParts.label: 3. The Fall and Passion
notes: The XML uses one LG for 108 lines.
contentParts.summary: A poem on the Fall and Christ's Passion.

contentParts.label: 4. The Ten Commandments
notes: The XML has forty long L rows; DIMEV describes twenty quatrains and notes two verse lines written per manuscript line.
contentParts.summary: A moral poem on the commandments, including swearing and Sunday trading.

contentParts.label: 5. Fragment on the Seven Sins
notes: Ten numbered six-line stanzas are followed by a large unnumbered group whose line numbering restarts; the text ends abruptly.
contentParts.summary: A discontinuous and incomplete moral poem on the seven deadly sins.

contentParts.label: 6. Christ on the Cross (fragment)
notes: Furnivall's one numbered item combines two separately indexed Middle English verse texts and two Latin prose authorities; it is not modeled as one medieval composition.
contentParts.summary: A composite numbered item joining two devotional poems on the crucified Christ with Latin scriptural framing.

contentParts.label: 7. A Rhyme-Beginning Fragment
notes: Three quatrains; DIMEV treats the passage as possibly the conclusion of a longer work.
contentParts.summary: A twelve-line fragment whose successive lines begin with the preceding line's rhyme.

contentParts.label: 8. A Moral Ode

locators.value: Egerton MS 613, ff. 7r–12v, collated with ff. 64r–70v

notes: The 199 XML LG elements are couplets; 258 NOTE elements carry the extensive same-codex variant apparatus.
contentParts.summary: The early Middle English Conduct of Life or Poema Morale, printed from one Egerton 613 copy with variants from the later copy in the same codex.

contentParts.label: 9. St Dunstan
notes: The edition's tailnote names intervening legends but does not encode them.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Dunstan.

contentParts.label: 10. An Oxford Student
notes: A complete 74-line selected miracle.
contentParts.summary: A South English Legendary miracle of the Virgin concerning an Oxford scholar.

contentParts.label: 11. The Jews and the Cross
notes: Twelve couplets in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A South English Legendary miracle involving Jews and the Cross.

contentParts.label: 12. St Swithin
notes: Eighty couplets in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Swithin.

contentParts.label: 13. St Kenelm
notes: The XML has 367 L rows while the printed milestone sequence reaches 368; the discrepancy is preserved as an encoding metric, not silently repaired.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Kenelm.

contentParts.label: 14. A Miracle of St James

locators.value: Harley MS 2277, selected passage at ff. 97r–101r

notes: This is an excerpt from the manuscript life, not the whole legend.
contentParts.summary: A selected miracle from the South English Legendary life of St James.

contentParts.label: 15. St Christopher
notes: A 228-line selection in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Christopher.

contentParts.label: 16. The 11,000 Virgins
notes: A 180-line selection in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary account of the Eleven Thousand Virgins.

contentParts.label: 17. St Edmund the Confessor
notes: The XML has 600 L rows and an inserted starred milestone 375*; later printed line numbers are therefore not a simple count of XML rows.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Edmund of Abingdon, archbishop and confessor.

contentParts.label: 18. St Edmund the King
notes: Fifty-one couplets in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Edmund, king and martyr.

contentParts.label: 19. St Katherine
notes: The XML has 309 L rows but the printed sequence ends at 305 after editorially irregular numbering.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Katherine.

contentParts.label: 20. St Andrew
notes: The selected text ends imperfectly.
contentParts.summary: An imperfect South English Legendary life of St Andrew following St Katherine.

contentParts.label: 21. Seinte Lucie
notes: Eighty-seven couplets in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A selected South English Legendary life of St Lucy.

contentParts.label: 22. St Edward: A Miracle of St John

locators.value: Harley MS 2277, selected passage beginning around f. 195

notes: The edition extracts one miracle rather than transcribing the whole source life.
contentParts.summary: A twenty-two-line selected miracle concerning St Edward and St John.

contentParts.label: 23. Judas Iscariot
notes: Seventy-three couplets in one XML LG.
contentParts.summary: A South English Legendary account of Judas Iscariot.

contentParts.label: 24. Pilate
notes: The XML has 261 L rows and a starred milestone 60*; DIMEV's reported end at f. 234 conflicts with the cataloged codex extent of f. 232v.
contentParts.summary: A South English Legendary account of Pilate.

contentParts.label: 25. A Song of Mercy

locators.value: Add MS 22283, f. 128vb–129ra; collated with Vernon MS f. 407ra–c

notes: Furnivall supplies four lines from Vernon and records eighty variant notes; this is the only one of items 25–30 that he collated against Vernon.
contentParts.summary: A sixteen-stanza moral song whose refrain says that mercy passes all things.

contentParts.label: 26. A Song of Deo Gracias
notes: Eleven eight-line stanzas; Furnivall explicitly says he did not compare this or the following songs with Vernon.
contentParts.summary: An eleven-stanza moral song with a Latin Deo gracias refrain.

contentParts.label: 27. Deus Caritas
notes: Seven eight-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A seven-stanza religious song with a Latin opening and refrain.

contentParts.label: 28. Another Song of Deo Gracias
notes: Six eight-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A six-stanza moral and devotional song with Latin refrain.

contentParts.label: 29. A Song: Know Thyself
notes: Nine twelve-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A nine-stanza moral song urging self-knowledge.

contentParts.label: 30. A Song of Yesterday
notes: Fifteen twelve-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A fifteen-stanza moral meditation on transience.

contentParts.label: 31. Why I Can't Be a Nun

locators.value: Cotton MS Vespasian D IX, ff. 177–182 and misbound f. 190

notes: The witness begins and ends imperfectly; the dislocated damaged leaf is part of the same medieval text.
contentParts.summary: An imperfect mid-fifteenth-century satirical complaint about female religious life.

contentParts.label: 32. Old Age
notes: The XML's nineteen long typographic L rows contain about seventy-eight short verse lines; L count is not a prosodic line count.
contentParts.summary: A first-person complaint on bodily decline in old age.

contentParts.label: 33. Earth
notes: Seven English six-line stanzas alternate with seven Latin rendering stanzas; the XML's Latin rows compress multiple metrical units.
contentParts.summary: A bilingual meditation on death in alternating Middle English and Latin stanzas.

contentParts.label: 34. Of Men Lif That Woneth in Land
notes: Twenty six-line stanzas; no named authorship is assigned from the manuscript's broader Kildare context.
contentParts.summary: A twenty-stanza social satire on the people of Kildare.

contentParts.label: 35. The Land of Cokaygne
notes: The XML has 191 L elements because the bracketed editorial statement '[Here a few lines seem to be lost.]' occupies one L; DIMEV describes ninety-five couplets.
contentParts.summary: A satirical Middle English vision of an earthly paradise and corrupt religious life.

contentParts.label: 36. Five Evil Things
notes: This item shares printed p. 161 with the end of item 35 and therefore has no separate PB milestone in the XML.
contentParts.summary: A seven-line gnomic list of five forms of bad rule or conduct.

scope.description: Two Conduct of Life copies in Egerton MS 613
Selected South English Legendary texts in Harley MS 2277
Selected Kildare Lyrics in Harley MS 913
Furnivall's selection, transcription, arrangement, and apparatus

extent.description: The body contains 36 numbered DIV2 items, 348 LG elements, and 5,615 L elements. These are encoding metrics: many long poems occupy one LG; item 32 compresses about 78 short lines into 19 typographic L rows; item 33 compresses Latin metrical text; item 35 includes an editorial loss notice as one L; and items 5, 13, 17, 19, 24, and 31 have resets, gaps, starred numbers, or offsets. The XML also contains 375 NOTE elements and 163 body PB milestones; item 36 shares printed p. 161 and has no separate PB.

formStatements.description: A verse anthology; item 6 embeds two short Latin prose authorities, and modern prose editorial apparatus is not treated as the form of the medieval selection.

scope.description: Latin headings, authorities, refrains, and alternating rendering stanzas
Furnivall's titles, headnotes, tailnotes, notes, and supplied loss statement

notes: Only item 25 is collated against the Vernon Manuscript; Furnivall explicitly says he had no time to compare item 26 and the following songs with the Bodleian copy.
The complete Internet Archive scan contains a duplicated capture sequence for printed pp. 54–55; this is a reproduction-layer defect and does not create duplicate XML PB milestones or medieval text.
The XML's 375 notes are highly uneven: item 8 has 258 same-codex variant notes and item 25 has 80 Vernon collation notes.
The review entry dated 2016-08-25 records a later validation and structural proofing pass, not the date of medieval composition, nineteenth-century editing, or initial electronic keying.

next_steps: Locate the U-M source-copy catalog record or imaging provenance.
Compare a complete Transactions of the Philological Society 1858 Part II issue against the standalone 1862 scan leaf by leaf.
Retain the volume year 1858 and publication year 1862 separately until the issue history is documented.
openQuestions.question: What exact physical issue or bound Transactions exemplar supplied the XML's half-title and 'Transactions ... 1858' title leaf, and how does it relate bibliographically to the complete standalone 1862 title-page state?

next_steps: Research each DIMEV record and textual tradition independently.
Distinguish composition, surviving witness, scribal copying, Furnivall transcription, and later catalog attribution.
Do not assign Michael of Kildare to item 34 or the whole Harley 913 group without item-specific evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which individual creators, composition dates, and original dialect regions can be assigned to the thirty-six medieval components without transferring witness-level evidence to abstract works?

next_steps: Inspect complete manuscript images or obtain a repository foliation statement for the final leaves.
Reconcile DIMEV's end at f. 234 with the current BL record's codex extent through f. 232v and any old foliation.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact end locus of item 24, Pilate, in Harley MS 2277?

scope.description: Probable origin of the relevant Egerton 613 sections
Probable production context of Harley MS 913
Probable dialect/origin of the Simeon Manuscript
Cataloged origin of the relevant Cotton manuscript portion

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English poets and compilers
notes: No component is assigned to Michael of Kildare merely because it survives in Harley MS 913.

scope.description: The thirty-six medieval components, without implying a single author
Selection and ordering of thirty-six items from five principal witnesses
Direct copying of the selected manuscript texts
Egerton 613's later Conduct of Life copy for item 8 and the Vernon witness for item 25

summaries.value: Furnivall's anthology ranges from sermons, moral lyrics, eschatological and Passion fragments, and the early Conduct of Life to sixteen selected South English Legendary narratives, six late-fourteenth-century religious songs, an imperfect anti-nunnery satire, and five Kildare Lyrics including Old Age, Earth, a satire on the people of Kildare, The Land of Cokaygne, and Five Evil Things. Its chief concerns are Christian conduct, death and judgment, Passion devotion, saints and miracles, mercy, transience, and satirical criticism of social and religious life. The collection is valuable both for the varied medieval verse it assembles and for the visible nineteenth-century practices of manuscript selection, expansion, punctuation, supply, and limited collation.

The encoded body has thirty-six numbered items on printed pages 1–161: seven Harley MS 913 items; Conduct of Life from two copies in Egerton MS 613; sixteen South English Legendary selections from Harley MS 2277; six songs from Add MS 22283, with Vernon collation only for item 25; Why I Can't Be a Nun from Cotton MS Vespasian D IX; and five further Harley MS 913 items. The XML omits the 1862 standalone title page, preface, contents, corrigenda, notes on pp. 162–165, index on pp. 166–171, and list of words on pp. 172–180 preserved in the complete scan.

The cataloged unit is Furnivall's edited selection, not a hypothetical medieval anthology. Numbered editorial parts do not erase separately indexed works, excerpts, fragments, embedded Latin, discontiguous manuscript leaves, variant witnesses, or imperfect boundaries. Witness dates and dialect regions are first-attestation evidence rather than automatically composition dates. XML L and LG counts are reproducible encoding metrics, not uniformly prosodic line and stanza counts.

titles.value: Early English Poems and Lives of Saints
Early English Poems and Lives of Saints (with Those of the Wicked Birds Pilate and Judas)

notes: This is the half-title encoded before the issue title leaf; it does not describe only items 9–24.
titles.value: The Early South-English Legendary or Lives of Saints

notes: The XML preserves this issue title leaf; direct scan and DIMEV evidence place Part II's Berlin publication in 1862.

work.summary: Furnivall's anthology ranges from sermons, moral lyrics, eschatological and Passion fragments, and the early Conduct of Life to sixteen selected South English Legendary narratives, six late-fourteenth-century religious songs, an imperfect anti-nunnery satire, and five Kildare Lyrics including Old Age, Earth, a satire on the people of Kildare, The Land of Cokaygne, and Five Evil Things. Its chief concerns are Christian conduct, death and judgment, Passion devotion, saints and miracles, mercy, transience, and satirical criticism of social and religious life. The collection is valuable both for the varied medieval verse it assembles and for the visible nineteenth-century practices of manuscript selection, expansion, punctuation, supply, and limited collation.
